title: Multi-horizon discrete-time hazard head plus rank-preserving blend
summary: Discrete-time survival work shows that next-event timing can be reframed as person-period binary hazards, letting standard classifiers model conditional event risk across multiple future intervals.
rationale: Discrete-time survival work shows that next-event timing can be reframed as person-period binary hazards, letting standard classifiers model conditional event risk across multiple future intervals. For this task, a 1-lap label alone may be too brittle; learning hazards for 1, 2, and 3 laps ahead can produce a smoother stopping-intent signal and a stronger ensemble member.
implementation_hint: Create row-causal targets for `pit in 1`, `pit in <=2`, and `pit in <=3` laps within each driver-race sequence. Train shared-feature classifiers or a person-period hazard model, then convert to next-lap probability and blend by OOF rank averaging with the current best model. Use `CalibratedClassifierCV` only on OOF predictions if the blend needs smoother probability shape; keep the final combination rank-focused because ROC AUC is the metric.
expected_effect: Adds a complementary timing model that captures stop imminence instead of just binary threshold crossings, often improving marginal cases one or two laps before the actual stop.
risk: Person-period expansion can explode row count and class imbalance. Calibration can flatten useful extremes or introduce ties if applied carelessly; all horizons and blend weights must be OOF.
